source_intake_record.raw_question_text: In a monovalent metal, one electron per atom is free to roam throughout the object. Evidently the energy of the composite structure must be less than the energy of the isolated atoms. This problem offers a crude but illuminating explanation for the cohesiveness of metals. When these atoms come together to form a metal, we get $N$ electrons in a much larger infinite square well of width $Na$. Because of the Pauli exclusion principle (which we will discuss in Chapter 5) there can only be one electron (two, if you include spin, but let's ignore that) in each allowed state. What is the lowest energy for this system?

multi_step: solving requires modeling N electrons in an infinite square well of width Na
must apply the one-electron-per-state constraint from the Pauli exclusion statement and fill the lowest N states
must sum the energy levels E_n proportional to n^2 from n=1 to N and simplify to the closed form
multiple answer choices suggest comparison against a derived expression rather than single-step recall
verifiability: answer is a specific closed-form formula with explicit symbols and coefficients
the problem is multiple choice with one option exactly matching the provided answer_text
derivation can be checked symbolically using the standard infinite-square-well spectrum and the sum of squares formula
answer_present=true and registry_passed=true support downstream checking
